mnemonics.title: SRS Characteristics
mnemonics.content: C-U-C-C-V-M-T: Correct, Unambiguous, Complete, Consistent, Verifiable, Modifiable, Traceable

mnemonics.title: Requirement Engineering Phases
mnemonics.content: E-A-S-V: Elicit, Analyze, Specify, Validate - Remember 'EASy Specification Validation'

mnemonics.title: Non-Functional Requirements
mnemonics.content: P-S-R-U-M-P: Performance, Security, Reliability, Usability, Maintainability, Portability - 'Please Serve Really Useful Main Program'

acronyms.usage: The formal document describing all requirements of the software system
The overall process of developing software from planning to maintenance

acronyms.expansion: Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers
acronyms.usage: Organization that defines standards like IEEE 830 for SRS
Quality attributes specifying how the system performs (not what it does)
Specific functions and features the system must perform

visualTips.visualization: Draw a table: Left column = 'What the system DOES' (Functional), Right column = 'HOW the system WORKS' (Non-Functional). Example: Login button = Functional; Login takes <2 seconds = Non-Functional

Think of a checklist: C(orrect) - facts checked, U(nambiguous) - single meaning, C(omplete) - nothing missing, C(onsistent) - no conflicts, V(erifiable) - can be tested, M(odifiable) - can be changed, T(raceable) - can be linked

Picture 5 people: 1) Interviewer asking questions, 2) Observer watching users, 3) Workshop with group, 4) Person analyzing documents, 5) Designer showing prototype - IAODP (Interview, Observation, Analysis, Document, Prototype)
Three circles: Users (who will use), Developers (who will build), Business (who pays/wants profit). All overlap in project success.

analogies.analogy: Just like construction needs blueprints before building, software development needs SRS before coding. You can't build a house without plans, and you can't build software without requirements.
Functional is the CONTENT of a car (transports you from A to B), Non-Functional is the QUALITY (speed, safety rating, fuel efficiency). Both are essential.
Elicitation is like a job interview - you ask questions, observe behavior, gather information about what the 'candidate' (stakeholder) needs and expects.
SRS is a contract between the development team and stakeholders - it clearly defines what's expected, preventing disputes later (like a marriage certificate defines expectations).
